turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Discussing Study or Career Plans with an Advisor

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Hi. I have an appointment about my study plan.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Yes, welcome. Please take a seat. I see your booking here. Before we start, can you tell me what you’d like to get out of today’s meeting?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Study plan, but also… future. Like job. I’m confused.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s completely fine, and very common. We can definitely talk about both. Let’s start with your studies, then connect that to career options. What program are you currently enrolled in?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Business. First year.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Alright, first-year Business. And how are you finding it so far? Is it matching what you expected?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Some parts yes. Some parts no. I thought more… practical.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That makes sense. First year can feel theoretical before things become more applied. Which subjects feel the least practical to you right now?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Accounting. Also economics. Too many graphs.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: I hear that a lot. When you say “too many graphs”, is it the maths side, or understanding what the graphs mean?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Meaning. Like… why this graph matters.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s a very important question. Often the “why” isn’t explained well at the beginning. Now, thinking ahead, do you have any idea what kind of work you might want after graduation, even loosely?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Not really. Office job maybe. But not boring office.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s fair. Most people don’t want boring. When you say “not boring”, do you mean more people-focused, creative, or something else?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: People. Talking. But also stable income.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That combination actually fits several roles, like marketing coordination, client support, project roles, or account management. Have you had any work experience so far?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Café job. Customer service.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s very relevant, even if it doesn’t feel academic. Now, can I ask: are you enjoying the café work, or is it just for money?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Both. I like talking to customers. But pay is low.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s honest, and useful information. It suggests you enjoy interaction, which is important for many business roles. Now, about your study plan, are you enrolled full-time this semester?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes. Four subjects. But I’m tired.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Four subjects can definitely feel heavy, especially in first year. Are you considering reducing your load, or just feeling overwhelmed right now?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: I was thinking… maybe three subjects. But will that delay graduation?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Good question. Yes, reducing your load can extend your degree slightly, but many students choose that for wellbeing reasons. It’s not a failure. We’d just need to check visa implications before making changes.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Visa is important. If I change subject or load, is that risky?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Minor adjustments within your degree are usually okay, but anything significant should be checked first. I recommend speaking with the international student office before making a decision.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Okay. Another question… if I don’t like accounting, can I avoid it later?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: You may still need some core subjects, but later you’ll have electives that align more with your interests. We can plan that strategically.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: That makes me feel better.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Good. Before we finish, is there something you’re worried about that we haven’t talked about yet?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Grades. If my grades are not high, can I still get job?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s a very common worry. Grades matter, but employers also value skills, experience, and communication. Your café work already gives you an advantage there.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Okay. So not only grades.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Exactly. Let me summarise briefly. You’re in first-year Business, finding some subjects too theoretical, interested in people-focused but stable work, and considering workload adjustments. For now, we’ll keep your current enrolment, and I’ll email you resources on academic support, career exploration, and visa advice. Does that sound right?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes. That sounds right.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Great. You’re asking the right questions at the right time.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Thank you. Week 4
